text: Design patterns are reusable solutions to commonly occurring problems in software design. They are not code to copy — they are templates for thinking about structure. Patterns are grouped into three categories: Creational (how objects are created), Structural (how objects are composed), and Behavioral (how objects communicate).

Creational Patterns — Factory: Defines an interface for creating objects but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Useful when the exact type of object needed is determined at runtime. In Go, factory functions are idiomatic — a function that returns an interface allows callers to work with behavior without knowing the concrete type.

Creational Patterns — Singleton: Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point. In Go, package-level variables initialized with sync.Once are the idiomatic singleton pattern. Use sparingly — singletons introduce global state that makes testing harder.

Structural Patterns — Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect. Bridges incompatible interfaces. In Go, wrapping a third-party type in a struct that implements your own interface is a common adapter pattern — keeping external dependencies at the boundary of your system.

Structural Patterns — Decorator: Adds behavior to an object dynamically without modifying its structure. In Go, HTTP middleware is a classic decorator pattern — wrapping handlers to add logging, authentication, or rate limiting without changing the handler itself.

Behavioral Patterns — Strategy: Defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. The algorithm can vary independently from the clients that use it. In Go, passing a function or interface as a parameter achieves this naturally — the caller decides the behavior.

Behavioral Patterns — Observer: Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, all dependents are notified. In Go, channels are a natural observer mechanism — goroutines subscribe to a channel and react when values are sent. Event-driven systems rely heavily on this pattern.

Behavioral Patterns — Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, logging, and undoable operations. In Go, representing commands as structs with an Execute method enables building task queues, undo systems, and audit logs cleanly.

Anti-patterns are common solutions that seem reasonable but make systems worse over time. God Object: a single component that knows too much and does too much — violates SRP. Premature Optimization: optimizing before profiling — wastes time and obscures intent. Shotgun Surgery: a single change requires modifications in many unrelated places — signals poor cohesion.
